data.title: SGX Open Slightly Lower As Regional Sentiment Tracks Wall Street Record Highs
data.description:
Singapore equities opened marginally lower, with the Straits Times Index (STI) slipping 0.15% to 5,000.16 at 9.09 am, as investors balanced Wall Street’s record-setting rally against cautious risk sentiment in Asian trading.

The STI was down 7.67 points in early trade, with mixed breadth across the Singapore market. On the broader board, 120 counters advanced against 97 decliners, suggesting selective buying despite the index weakness. Early trading volume stood at S$227.56 million across 162.56 million shares.

Regional cues remained supportive after US equities closed at fresh highs overnight, driven by easing geopolitical tensions and expectations of diplomatic progress in the Middle East. The S&P 500 rose 0.26% while the Nasdaq added 0.36%, extending its winning streak to 12 straight sessions, the longest since 2009.

Sentiment was lifted after reports of a 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon and remarks from US President Donald Trump indicating potential talks with Iran over the weekend. Market participants also weighed mixed US economic data, including a larger-than-expected drop in jobless claims, which pointed to labour market resilience.

In Singapore, index heavyweights were mostly subdued in early trade. DBS slipped 0.09% to S$57.25 while SGX edged down to S$21.08. UOB was flat at S$37.45, while OCBC traded at S$22.67. Singtel held at S$4.81, and Keppel eased to S$11.92.

In derivatives, activity remained muted in early trade across key contracts, with MSCI Singapore Index Futures last at 452.55 and FTSE China A50 Index Futures at 15,442.

data.title: Stock Today: SD Guthrie Trades Flat Amid Muted Activity
data.description:
SD Guthrie Bhd traded flat at RM5.99 as at 2.46 pm, with no change recorded from its last closing price as market activity remained subdued.

The plantation counter opened at RM6.00 and moved within a narrow range, touching a high of RM6.00 and a low of RM5.98 during the session. A total of 3.42 million shares were traded, reflecting relatively modest investor participation.

Market depth showed selling interest slightly outweighing buying momentum, with 858,900 shares on the sell side compared with 52,100 shares queued to buy at RM5.99.

The muted price action comes amid a lack of fresh catalysts, although the group recently announced a board-related update. Earlier this week, SD Guthrie appointed Ariff Ismail as alternate director to Mohd Irwan Ahmad Mustafa, effective April 15.

Ariff, who is currently Head of Cluster in Strategic Investments at Permodalan Nasional Berhad, brings over a decade of experience in managing strategic and core investments across multiple sectors.

Despite the corporate development, the stock has largely held steady in recent sessions, suggesting investors are taking a wait-and-see approach as the plantation sector continues to track broader commodity trends.

data.title: Stock Today: Mah Sing Edges Higher On Steady Buying Interest As Property Sentiment Firms
data.description:
Mah Sing Group Bhd traded slightly higher in late afternoon trade, supported by steady buying interest amid a broadly stable tone in the property sector.

As at 4.31pm, the stock rose 1 sen or 0.96% to RM1.05, with some 4.65 million shares changing hands. The counter opened at RM1.05 and moved within a tight range between RM1.03 and RM1.05 throughout the session.

Buying interest appeared firm at RM1.04, with bid volumes slightly outweighing immediate selling pressure at RM1.05, suggesting a balanced but mildly positive sentiment.

The modest gain comes as investors continue to reassess property counters, which have seen selective accumulation in recent weeks amid expectations of stable demand conditions and ongoing developer activity in key urban areas.

Over a broader period, Mah Sing has gradually recovered from levels around RM1.02 in November 2025, reflecting a slow but steady improvement in sentiment despite uneven market momentum.

Trading activity remained healthy, indicating continued retail participation in the counter even as the wider property sector tracks a cautious but stable trajectory.

data.title: Kospi Falls 0.55% On Profit-Taking, Winning Streak Ends
data.description:
South Korean equities snapped a three-session rally on April 17, with investors locking in gains and turning cautious amid renewed diplomatic signals over potential US-Iran peace talks.

The benchmark Kospi slipped 34.13 points, or 0.55%, to finish at 6,191.92, retreating from its previous trading session that closed above the 6,200 level, its highest since the outbreak of the US-Iran conflict on Feb. 28. The index’s all-time closing peak remains 6,307.27, set on Feb. 26.

Market sentiment was also shaped by geopolitical headlines, as US President Donald Trump struck an optimistic tone on possible renewed negotiations with Iran over the weekend, while Israel and Lebanon announced a 10-day ceasefire.

Trading activity was steady, with volume reaching 680.2 billion shares worth 22.8 trillion won (US$15.4 billion). Decliners slightly outpaced advancers, 463 to 389.

Foreign investors led the sell-off with a net outflow of two trillion won, while institutions bought a net 150.3 billion won and retail investors added 1.4 trillion won.

Blue-chip stocks largely weakened. Samsung Electronics fell 0.69% to 216,000 won, while SK hynix dropped 2.34% to 1.1 million won. Defensive sectors were hit harder, with Hanwha Aerospace plunging 6.32% to 1.4 million won and Korea Aerospace Industries slipping 0.79%.

Energy and biotech names also came under pressure, including Doosan Enerbility easing 2.08% and Samsung Bioepis Holdings declining 2.96%). Internet and gaming stocks were softer, with Naver down 0.96% and NC retreating 1.5%.

However, not all sectors closed in the red. Hyundai Motor gained 0.75% to 538,000 won, while shipbuilder HD Hyundai Heavy Industries surged 4.15%, standing out as one of the session’s strongest performers.

Overall, the market reflected a pause in momentum as geopolitical developments and profit-taking tempered recent gains.

data.title: Bitcoin Stalls Below US$75,000 As Ethereum Slips
data.description:
Bitcoin hovered just shy of the key US$75,000 threshold on April 17, caught in a tight consolidation band as investors weighed macro risks against steady institutional inflows.

The world’s largest cryptocurrency has been “coiling” near resistance, with price action reflecting indecision after a recent push toward the psychological ceiling.

While Bitcoin held its ground, Ethereum edged lower in recent sessions, underperforming its larger peer and reinforcing a growing divergence between the two dominant digital assets.

However, the broader picture suggests a market pausing rather than reversing. Bitcoin’s climb from roughly US$71,000 levels earlier in the week to the mid-US$74,000 range highlights underlying demand, supported in part by continued inflows into spot ETFs.

Yet momentum is fragile as analysts point to US$75,000 as a critical breakout level, one that has repeatedly capped rallies. Failure to decisively clear it could keep prices range-bound in the near term, while a confirmed breakout may open the path toward higher targets.

Ethereum’s softer tone, meanwhile, adds another layer of caution. Despite earlier rebounds this month, the asset has struggled to sustain upward momentum, reflecting weaker investor conviction relative to Bitcoin and ongoing underperformance in 2026.

Macro forces remain the dominant driver. Easing geopolitical tensions and risk-on sentiment in equities have lent support to crypto, but uncertainty still clouds directional conviction.

data.title: Netflix Shares Slide On Weak Outlook, Co-Founder Reed Hastings To Step Down
data.description:
Netflix shares fell sharply in early European trading on Friday after the streaming giant issued a weaker-than-expected revenue forecast and announced that chairman and co-founder Reed Hastings would not seek re-election.

Frankfurt-listed shares of the company dropped 8.7% by 0603 GMT, reflecting investor disappointment over the softer outlook despite a strong run earlier this year.

The decline came after Netflix signalled that revenue growth could come in below market expectations, raising concerns over its near-term earnings trajectory even as the broader streaming industry remains competitive.

The leadership development also weighed on sentiment, with Hastings, a long-time figure at the company, confirming he would step away from the board, marking a significant shift in Netflix’s leadership structure.

Despite the sell-off, Netflix shares have gained about 15% year-to-date in New York, highlighting that the latest pullback comes after a period of strong performance.

The dual impact of a weaker outlook and leadership transition underscores growing investor sensitivity to both earnings visibility and management continuity in the global tech sector.

Reuters

data.title: IPO Debut: Empire Premium Closes 49% Higher On Strong Main Market Debut
data.description:
Newly listed Empire Premium Food Bhd made a strong entrance on Bursa Malaysia, closing its first trading day up 48.6% as robust investor demand drove heavy trading volumes.

The stock ended at RM1.04, a gain of 34 sen from its IPO price of 70 sen, with a hefty 266.5 million shares changing hands, placing it among the most actively traded counters of the day.

Momentum was evident from the opening bell, with the stock debuting at RM1.10 and surging as much as 57.14% in early trade. It later touched an intraday high of RM1.21 before easing, with a low of 99 sen recorded during the session.

By midday, Empire Premium was still holding firm at RM1.06 on 217.3 million shares traded, underscoring sustained buying interest throughout the day.

The strong debut reflects continued appetite for IPOs on Bursa Malaysia, particularly for consumer-linked plays, as investors chase growth stories with scalable business models.

Overall, Empire Premium’s stellar debut signals healthy IPO sentiment and ample liquidity in the market, though sustaining such gains will depend on the company’s ability to deliver on earnings growth post-listing.

Meanwhile, BusinessToday had the opportunity to interview Empire Premium Chief Executive Officer Nicole Lim regarding the company’s market debut, and this was her response.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Edges Higher At Midday On Upbeat GDP Signal, But Breadth Stays Weak
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia clawed into positive territory by midday on April 17, buoyed by stronger-than-expected economic signals, though underlying sentiment remained cautious with decliners still outpacing gainers.

At 12:30pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) rose 1.53 points, or 0.09%, to 1,691.24, reversing earlier losses after the Department of Statistics Malaysia projected the economy to expand 5.3% in the first quarter of 2026.

The modest rebound in the benchmark index was mirrored across broader indices, with FBM 70, FBM Emas and FBM Shariah gauges also inching higher, suggesting selective buying interest despite a lack of strong conviction.

Trading activity was brisk, with 1.88 billion shares changing hands worth RM1.4 billion, as retail-driven counters dominated the most active list.

Among them, Empire Premium Food Bhd surged 36 sen to RM1.06, emerging as both the top active and one of the top gainers, while Borneo Oil Bhd and Zetrix AI Bhd also saw heavy trading volumes.

On the blue-chip front, United Plantations Bhd led gainers, climbing 72 sen to RM34.28, followed by Allianz Malaysia Bhd and Fraser & Neave Holdings Bhd, reflecting pockets of strength in defensive and consumer-linked stocks.

However, losses in selected heavyweights capped broader upside. Malaysian Pacific Industries Bhd fell 30 sen to RM33.68, while Petronas Dagangan Bhd and Dutch Lady Milk Industries Bhd also retreated.

Despite the headline gains, market breadth painted a more fragile picture, with 471 losers outnumbering 427 gainers, underscoring lingering caution among investors.

Overall, the stronger GDP outlook offered a timely lift to Bursa’s benchmark, but with selling still broad-based, the market’s midday rebound looks more like a tentative bounce than a decisive shift in sentiment.

data.title: Bursa Opens Lower As Blue Chips Slip, Tracking Softer Oil And Mixed Wall Street Tone
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia started Friday’s session in the red, with the FBM KLCI edging lower in early trade as weakness in heavyweight counters and softer commodity cues weighed on sentiment.

As at 9.32am, the FBM KLCI stood at 1,684.51, down 5.20 points or 0.31%. The index moved between 1,682.88 and 1,689.07 in early trading.

Broader market indices also trended lower. The FBM70 fell 47.50 points or 0.27% to 17,594.17, while the FBM EMAS slipped 36.84 points or 0.30% to 12,434.78. The FBM Shariah Index declined 43.56 points or 0.35% to 12,363.28, while the FBM Emas Shariah Index eased 3.20 points or 0.32% to 1,002.87.

Sentiment was partly influenced by softer crude oil prices, which retreated in early Asian trade as easing geopolitical tensions and renewed hopes of US-Iran talks reduced concerns over supply disruptions. The move came after a 10-day ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel took effect, cooling recent risk premiums in energy markets.

Global cues were also mixed after Wall Street closed at fresh record highs overnight, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq extending gains on optimism over easing Middle East tensions. However, analysts noted that momentum remains sensitive to shifting headlines, with investors still focused on geopolitical developments and economic signals.

Locally, market breadth was uneven in early trade, with selective buying in defensive and consumer names offset by selling pressure in index-heavy sectors.

Among index constituents, banks were slightly softer, with DBS edging lower while OCBC Bank and UOB traded with limited movement. Singtel and Keppel were broadly steady.

Empire Premium Food Bhd remained in focus, continuing its post-IPO momentum with active trading as it held well above its listing price. The counter was among the most heavily traded stocks in early session.

Other active counters included Zetrix, Toyoven and Armada, which saw mixed price action across small and mid-cap segments.

data.title: Hong Kong Stocks Close Lower As Hang Seng Slips 233 Points
data.description:
Hong Kong shares ended the trading session of April 17 on a weaker note, with the benchmark Hang Seng Index falling 0.89%, or 233 points, to close at 26,160, as selling pressure weighed on major tech and blue-chip counters.

Market turnover reached a solid HK$238 billion, reflecting active trading despite the broad-based decline. The Hang Seng Tech Index also slipped 0.97% to 5,042 points, underscoring continued weakness in the technology sector.

Among heavyweight stocks, Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd dropped 3%, while insurer AIA, collectible-toy maker Pop Mart and Zijin International also each lost around 3%. In contrast, Alibaba managed to buck the trend, edging up 0.4% to provide a rare bright spot in an otherwise subdued session.

On the mainland, markets were mixed. The Shanghai Composite Index eased 0.1%, or 4.12 points, to 4,051, while the Shenzhen Component Index advanced 0.6% to 14,885.

The session reflected cautious sentiment across Greater China equities, with profit-taking and uneven sector performance keeping indexes under pressure despite steady liquidity.

data.title: Brent Crude Tumbles To US$90, Stocks And Bonds Jump After Iran Says Straits Of Hormuz Open
data.description:
The price of oil tumbled and stocks and government bonds rallied on Friday after Iran’s foreign minister said that the Strait of Hormuz was open for passage while a ceasefire is in force.

Abbas Araqchi said in a post on X that passage for all commercial vessels through the Strait of Hormuz was declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire, in line with the ceasefire in Lebanon.The price of benchmark Brent crude futures tumbled to $90 a barrel, down 9 per cent on the day, U.S. crude fell 10 per cent to $81.5 a barrel.

While still above pre-war levels, that is down significantly from late March’s highs, which, for Brent, were close to $120 a barrel.Stocks around the world, which had already been trading around record highs, jumped further on the news. Europe’s broad STOXX 600 was last up 1.3 per cent having been close to flat before the announcement, S&P futures were 0.9 per cent higher.

“If we move to a situation where the path is still towards de-escalation – but we now have the bonus of commodity flows through Hormuz getting back to something resembling a normal level that we saw pre-conflict – then that’s obviously removing a pretty chunky tail risk for the economy,” said Michael Brown, senior research strategist at Pepperstone.

“I think that’s why markets are reacting so positively.”

Government bonds also rallied, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield dropping 7 basis points to 4.23 per cent, its lowest since mid March.Unlike stocks, which had already regained their pre-war highs in the United States and many other markets, bonds have yet to do so.

European bonds have sold off more than U.S. ones since the war began as the impact of higher energy prices had traders rushing in to price rate hikes from the European Central Bank and Bank of England.

On Friday they were outperforming as those rate cut bets were pared back.

Germany’s 10-year yield fell 8 basis points to 2.96 per cent, while the rate sensitive two-year yield fell 10 bps to 2.42 per cent. Britain’s two-year yield fell 15 bps to 4.10 per cent.

The dollar also slid, falling 0.6 per cent to 158 yen, while the euro was up 0.6 per cent at $1.1848, a two-month high.

Reuters

data.title: Stock Today: YTL Power Falls 2.4% Amid Selling Pressure, Energy Uncertainty Lingers
data.description:
YTL Power International Bhd slipped in active trading, tracking a softer tone across the broader utilities segment as investors remained cautious amid global energy price volatility.

As at 3.34 pm, the stock fell 9 sen or 2.42% to RM3.63, with some 11.96 million shares traded. The counter opened at RM3.72 and briefly touched an intraday high of RM3.72 before sliding to a low of RM3.59, reflecting selling pressure throughout the session.

Market activity showed a slight imbalance on the sell side, with offers at RM3.64 outweighing bids at RM3.63, suggesting near-term caution among traders.

The decline comes against a backdrop of heightened uncertainty in global energy markets, with oil price swings and geopolitical tensions continuing to influence sentiment towards power and utility-related counters.

Over a longer horizon, YTL Power has retreated from around RM3.95 in late October 2025, indicating some consolidation after a stronger run previously, as investors reassess earnings visibility and external cost pressures.

Despite the pullback, trading volumes remained elevated, pointing to sustained investor interest in the counter as the market digests ongoing developments in the global energy landscape.

data.title: Bursa Malaysia Closes Higher As Strong GDP Outlook Fuels Buying Momentum
data.description:
Bursa Malaysia ended the week on a stronger note, extending its upward momentum as upbeat economic data reinforced confidence in the country’s growth outlook despite global headwinds.

The benchmark FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) rose 5.50 points, or 0.33%, to close at 1,695.21, lifted by improved sentiment after the Department of Statistics Malaysia projected first-quarter 2026 GDP growth at a solid 5.3%.

The gains were broad-based, with the FBM 70 jumping 82.06 points (0.47%) to 17,723.73, while the FBM Emas and FBM Shariah indices also posted firm advances at 46.55 points (0.37%) and 41.22 points (0.33%) to 12,518.17 and 12,448.06, respectively, signalling renewed risk appetite across the market.

Investor sentiment was underpinned by the view that domestic demand remains resilient, even as external risks, particularly elevated oil prices linked to geopolitical tensions, continue to cloud the global outlook.

Trading activity, however, eased slightly, with total turnover slipping to 3.46 billion shares worth RM3.08 billion compared to 3.77 billion shares valued at RM3.46 billion in the previous session.

On the trading floor, Empire Premium Food Bhd led the most active list, surging 34 sen to RM1.04, followed by heavy volumes in Borneo Oil Bhd and Zetrix AI Bhd.

Other actively traded counters included MMAG Holdings Bhd, which slipped half a sen, and Capital A Bhd, which edged 1.5 sen higher.

Overall, strong GDP signals provided a timely boost to Bursa’s rally, but with turnover moderating and global risks lingering, the market’s upward trajectory is likely to remain measured rather than explosive.

data.title: Ringgit Extends Gains Against Dollar, Others
data.description:
The ringgit experienced gains against the US dollar and other major currencies on Friday, supported by strong domestic sentiment, according to Bernama.

Markets are anticipating Malaysia’s advance estimate of the first-quarter 2026 (1Q 2026) gross domestic product (GDP) at noon, which could further stabilize the local currency.

At 8 am, the local currency strengthened to 3.9505/9565 against the greenback from 3.9520/9560 at Thursday’s close.

Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the ringgit is expected to hold around RM3.95 today.

“All eyes are on the advance estimate for 1Q 2026 GDP, with median forecasts at 5.5 per cent (4Q 2025: 6.3 per cent). The data could support the ringgit,” he told Bernama.

On the global front, he said optimism over a second round of US–Iran talks and prospects for a permanent ceasefire could lift risk sentiment, supporting emerging market currencies, including the ringgit.

At the open, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.

It strengthened against the British pound to 5.3431/3512 from 5.3506/3560 at Thursday’s close, rose versus the euro to 4.6537/6608 from 4.6551/6598, and gained against the Japanese yen to 2.4812/4851 from 2.4848/4874.

The local currency traded mostly higher against its ASEAN peers.

It climbed against the Singapore dollar to 3.1026/1078 from 3.1069/1103 on Thursday, appreciated versus the Thai baht to 12.3222/3502 from 12.3473/3664, and edged up against the Philippine peso to 6.58/6.60 from 6.59/6.60, but was flat against the Indonesian rupiah at 230.5/230.9.

data.title: Plantations, Insurers Lead Rally As Gainers Outpace Losers
data.description:
The stock market movement closed on a firmer footing, with gainers outnumbering losers in a session marked by selective buying in plantation and financial heavyweights.

Market breadth turned positive with 611 gainers against 476 losers, while 608 counters ended unchanged, signalling improving sentiment despite pockets of weakness.

Leading the charge was United Plantations Bhd, which climbed 62 sen to RM34.18, followed by Allianz Malaysia Bhd, up 50 sen to RM21.20, reflecting continued interest in defensive and dividend-yielding plays.

Retail-driven momentum remained evident as Empire Premium Food Bhd surged 34 sen to RM1.04 on heavy volume, making it one of the most actively traded counters of the day. Consumer names such as Fraser & Neave Holdings Bhd and Ayer Holdings Bhd also featured among the top gainers.

On the downside, Petronas Dagangan Bhd led decliners, slipping 26 sen to RM20.58, while technology-related names such as KESM Industries Bhd and Frontken Corporation Bhd came under selling pressure.

Other laggards included Batu Kawan Bhd and Life Water, reflecting broader profit-taking across selected counters.

Overall, the positive breadth suggests a tentative return of risk appetite, but continued selling in key sectors indicates investors remain selective, balancing opportunities against lingering market uncertainties.

data.title: Singapore Stocks Slip As Caution Prevails
data.description:
Singapore equities ended lower on April 17, with the benchmark Straits Times Index slipping 0.2% to close at 4,997.27, as investors remained cautious amid mixed regional signals.

The muted decline reflects a market still in consolidation mode, with participants holding back from aggressive positioning in the absence of strong catalysts.

Broader sentiment remained fragile, weighed by external uncertainties including geopolitical developments and commodity price swings, which continue to shape risk appetite across Asian markets.

Analysts said the pullback underscores lingering caution among investors, even as selective buying persists in pockets of the market.

Looking ahead, the Singapore market is expected to track regional trends closely, with volatility likely to persist as investors await clearer global direction.

data.title: Stocks Reverse As Investors Await News On US-Iran Peace Talks
data.description:
Stock markets fell on Friday as investors awaited news of an extension to the Iran-US ceasefire, while crude prices edged back down following the previous day’s rally.

The losses follow a healthy, record-breaking week for equities fuelled by hopes the Middle East war, which is heading into a seventh week, could be close to an end after Donald Trump said negotiators were close to a deal.

But worries abound that a shaky truce agreed earlier this month — and which ends next week — could fall apart and spark a fresh market rout.

The US president on Thursday struck an optimistic tone, telling reporters that “it’s looking very good that we’re going to make a deal with Iran, and it’s going to be a good deal”, adding that talks between Washington and Tehran could resume this weekend.

He also claimed Iran had “agreed to give us back the nuclear dust”, using his name for the country’s enriched uranium stockpile, and the deal would include “free oil” as well as the opening of the Strait of Hormuz.

“We had to make sure that Iran never gets a nuclear weapon,” Trump said at the White House. “They’ve totally agreed to that. They’ve agreed to almost everything, so maybe if they can get to the table, there’s a difference.”

Iran has given no public indication it would surrender its stockpile.

However, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth took a tough line on the situation earlier in the day, telling a Pentagon news conference: “If Iran chooses poorly, then they will have a blockade and bombs dropping on infrastructure, power and energy.”

Meanwhile, some Gulf Arab and European leaders fear a long-term agreement could take six months to achieve and called for the truce to cover such a time period, Bloomberg reported.

They wanted the Strait of Hormuz — through which about a fifth of global oil and LNG passes — opened immediately and have warned in private of a global food crisis if that is not achieved by next month, the report said.

Stocks fell across the region, with Tokyo, which hit a record high Thursday, among the biggest losers, with Seoul, Hong Kong, Shanghai and Singapore also well down.

Taiwan’s TAIEX fell. On Thursday it hit a market capitalisation of US$4.14 trillion to top the UK’s market capitalisation and become the world’s seventh biggest, according to Bloomberg data.

That came even after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq enjoyed record closes on Wall Street.

Analysts said traders were heading into the weekend to positioning for any surprise developments.

Oil prices dropped more than one percent, a day after sharp gains, though both main contracts remain just below $100 a barrel.

There was some support from a 10-day ceasefire agreed between Israel and Lebanon that took effect at 2100 GMT Thursday.

Tel Aviv has sent troops into its northern neighbour since militant group Hezbollah launched rocket attacks in support of Iran last month.

Hezbollah has not officially said if it will recognise the ceasefire but one of its lawmakers told AFP on Thursday that the group would respect it if Israeli attacks on its militants stopped.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the 10-day ceasefire with Lebanon offered an opportunity for a “historic peace agreement”, but insisted that the disarmament of militant group Hezbollah remained a precondition.

Trump said he will invite the countries’ leaders to the White House.

“While investors remain buoyed by talks of an extension in the US-Iran ceasefire and an announced Israel-Lebanon 10 day ceasefire, risk sentiment remains fragile as an immediate deal remains unlikely given that the countries remain far apart on key issues,” wrote National Australia Bank’s Skye Masters.

Fiona Cincotta of City Index, said: “While risks remain — particularly around disruptions to key shipping routes such as the Strait of Hormuz — markets are increasingly pricing in a scenario where oil prices have peaked unless tensions re-escalate.”

But she warned “the outlook remains fragile. A breakdown in diplomacy or renewed escalation could quickly reverse recent gains”.

data.title: Foreign Exchange Rates April 17, 2026
data.description:
The closing foreign exchange rates, sourced from Bank Negara Malaysia and recorded at 5pm on April 17, 2026, provide a comprehensive overview of currency fluctuations for the day.

These rates, crucial for investors and businesses alike, reflect the relative strength or weakness of major global currencies against the Malaysian Ringgit. They serve as a vital indicator for assessing international trade competitiveness, investment opportunities and overall economic trends in the global market.

The exchange rates are as shown below:

data.title: Wall Street Ends at Record Highs as Oil Slumps on Strait of Hormuz Reopening
data.description:
US stocks closed sharply higher on April 17, with the benchmark S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite notching their third straight record finishes, as easing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and a plunge in oil prices lifted investor sentiment.

Reuters reported that the rally followed comments from Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi confirming that the Strait of Hormuz was “completely open” to commercial shipping after a ceasefire agreement in Lebanon.

Optimism was further boosted after US President Donald Trump signalled potential talks with Tehran over the weekend aimed at ending the ongoing conflict.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite rose 1.52% to 24,468.48, extending its winning streak to 13 sessions, its longest since 1992. The S&P 500 gained 1.2% to 7,126.06, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 1.79% to 49,447.43, marking its highest close since late February.

For the week, the S&P 500 advanced 4.53%, the Nasdaq surged 6.84%, and the Dow added 3.2%.

US crude prices tumbled more than 11% on hopes that shipping disruptions would ease, reducing inflationary pressure. The sharp drop weighed heavily on energy shares, with Exxon Mobil falling 3.6% and Chevron down 2.2%, making the sector the S&P 500’s worst performer with a 2.9% decline.

In contrast, consumer discretionary stocks led gains, rising nearly 2%, driven by cruise operators. Royal Caribbean jumped 7.3%, while Carnival Corporation gained 7%. Industrials also outperformed, up 1.8%, with United Airlines soaring 7%.

The small-cap Russell 2000 outpaced large caps, rising 2.1% to a record close.

Despite the broad rally, some corporate results tempered enthusiasm. Netflix plunged 9.7% after issuing weaker-than-expected earnings guidance and announcing the departure of co-founder Reed Hastings. Meanwhile, Alcoa dropped 6.8% after reporting disappointing quarterly results amid rising costs and soft demand.

While markets welcomed the reopening of the critical shipping route, analysts warned that logistical challenges could delay a full resumption of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery for global energy supplies.

Market breadth remained strong, with advancing stocks outnumbering decliners by more than 4-to-1 on the New York Stock Exchange. Trading volume was also robust, with over 20 billion shares exchanged, above the recent average.

Investors now turn their attention to the outcome of potential US-Iran talks, which could determine whether the current relief rally has further room to run.

data.title: Oil Prices Plunge 11% After Iran Agreed For Straits Of Hormuz Reopen
data.description:
Oil prices plunged on Friday after Iran’s foreign minister said the Strait of Hormuz was fully open to commercial traffic for the remainder of the 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon and President Trump said Iran had agreed to indefinitely suspend its nuclear program.

Futures on Brent crude, the international pricing benchmark, fell as much as 11% to trade below $89 per barrel before paring losses, while those on US benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude fell by as much as 11.1% to trade at $81 before regaining. Both products opened the week above $100.
